content: You are a doctor. You will be presented with patient cases. You may ask for labs, imaging, vitals, history, or exam findings. When ready, state your diagnosis and recommended next step.

New patient:

A 3-year-old boy is brought to the physician for evaluation of pallor and increasing lethargy for 3 days.

To evaluate this patient, I would first take a detailed history, including asking about any recent illnesses, dietary habits, family history of anemia or blood disorders, and any other symptoms such as fever, vomiting, or diarrhea. I would also perform a thorough physical examination, paying close attention to the skin color, heart and lung sounds, and any signs of anemia or infection.

Based on the presentation of pallor and increasing lethargy, I would suspect anemia as a possible diagnosis. To confirm this, I would order a complete blood count (CBC) to assess the hemoglobin, hematocrit, and red blood cell indices. If the CBC results indicate anemia, further tests such as reticulocyte count, iron studies, and possibly a bone marrow biopsy may be needed to determine the underlying cause.

In the meantime, I would recommend ensuring the child is well-hydrated and providing a balanced diet rich in iron, vitamin B12, and folate. If the child's condition worsens or if the CBC results indicate a severe anemia, I may consider a blood transfusion as a temporary measure while further investigations are conducted.

New patient:

A 40-year-old man visits the office with complaints of fever and abdominal pain for the past 6 days.

To evaluate this patient, I would first take a detailed history, including asking about any recent travel, exposure to sick individuals, dietary habits, and any other symptoms such as diarrhea, vomiting, or jaundice. I would also perform a thorough physical examination, paying close attention to the abdomen, skin color, and any signs of infection or inflammation.

Based on the presentation of fever and abdominal pain, I would suspect an infectious or inflammatory process as a possible diagnosis. To confirm this, I would order a complete blood count (CBC) to assess the white blood cell count and differential, as well as blood cultures to identify any potential bacterial infection. I would also order a comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) to evaluate liver and kidney function, and an abdominal ultrasound to assess for any structural abnormalities.

If the CBC results indicate leukocytosis with a left shift, and the blood cultures grow a pathogen, I would initiate appropriate antibiotic therapy based on the sensitivity pattern. If the CMP results show abnormal liver or kidney function, further investigations such as hepatitis panel, autoimmune markers, or imaging studies may be needed to determine the underlying cause.

In the meantime

Laboratory results:
Hemoglobin 16 g/dL
Hematocrit 44%
Leukocyte count 18,000/mm3
Neutrophils  60%
Bands 4%
Eosinophils 2%
Basophils 1%
Lymphocytes  27%
Monocytes 6%
Platelet count 345,000/mm3
Aspartate aminotransferase (AST)  57 IU/L
Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 70 IU/L
Alkaline phosphatase 140 U/L
Total bilirubin 8 mg/dL
Direct bilirubin 5 mg/dL
Vital signs: temperature 39.0°C (102.2°F), blood pressure 114/70 mm Hg, respiratory rate 17/min
Direct bilirubin 5 mg/dL
An ultrasound is also done to the patient which is shown in the picture
On physical examination, there is tenderness on palpation of the right upper quadrant

Based on the laboratory results and physical examination findings, the patient has leukocytosis with a left shift, elevated liver enzymes, and jaundice, which are suggestive of an infectious or inflammatory process affecting the liver. The ultrasound findings and right upper quadrant tenderness further support this diagnosis.

The differential diagnosis for this patient includes acute cholecystitis, hepatitis, or a liver abscess. To further narrow down the diagnosis, I would order an abdominal CT scan with contrast to assess for any gallstones, inflammation, or abscess formation. I would also consider obtaining a hepatitis panel, including hepatitis A, B, and C serologies, as well as autoimmune markers such as antinuclear antibodies (ANA) and anti-smooth muscle antibodies (ASMA) to evaluate for autoimmune hepatitis.

In the meantime, I would initiate empirical antibiotic therapy with a broad-spectrum antibiotic such as piperacillin-tazobactam or a combination of ceftriaxone and metronidazole to cover for potential biliary tract infection. I would also provide supportive care, including analgesics for pain management

New patient:

A 32-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital after undergoing an open cholecystectomy under general anesthesia.

To evaluate this patient, I would first take a detailed history, including asking about any previous surgeries, allergies, and any other medical conditions. I would also perform a thorough physical examination, paying close attention to the surgical site, vital signs, and any signs of infection or complications.

Based on the presentation of a postoperative patient, I would suspect potential complications such as infection, bleeding, or anesthesia-related issues as possible diagnoses. To confirm this, I would order a complete blood count (CBC) to assess the white blood cell count and differential, as well as blood cultures to identify any potential bacterial infection. I would also order a comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) to evaluate liver and kidney function, and a coagulation panel to assess for any bleeding disorders.

If the CBC results indicate leukocytosis with a left shift, and the blood cultures grow a pathogen, I would initiate appropriate antibiotic therapy based on the sensitivity pattern. If the CMP results show abnormal liver or kidney function, further investigations such as liver function tests, imaging studies, or consultation with a specialist may be needed to determine the underlying cause.

In the meantime, I would closely monitor the patient's vital signs, surgical site, and

Laboratory results:
Serum glucose (random) 174 mg/dL
Sodium 142 mEq/L
Potassium 3.9 mEq/L
Chloride 101 mEq/L
Serum creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Blood urea nitrogen 10 mg/dL
Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 150 U/L
Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 172 U/L
Serum bilirubin (total) 0.9 mg/dL
Vital signs: blood pressure 134/82 mm Hg, respiratory rate 16/min

Based on the laboratory results and clinical presentation, the patient has elevated blood glucose levels, which may be due to stress response or pre-existing diabetes. The liver enzymes (ALT and AST) are also elevated, which may indicate liver injury or inflammation. The serum bilirubin level is within normal limits, suggesting no significant biliary obstruction.

The differential diagnosis for this patient includes postoperative complications such as infection, liver injury, or anesthesia-related issues. To further narrow down the diagnosis, I would order a repeat CBC and blood cultures to assess for any ongoing infection. I would also consider obtaining a liver function panel, including alkaline phosphatase, gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT), and coagulation studies, to evaluate for any liver injury or bleeding disorders.

In the meantime, I would closely monitor the patient's vital signs, surgical site, and blood glucose levels. I would also provide supportive care, including analgesics for pain management and insulin therapy if needed to control blood glucose levels. New patient:

A 16-year-old male presents to his pediatrician with a sore throat. He reports a severely painful throat preceded by several days of malaise and fatigue.

To evaluate this patient, I would first take a detailed history, including asking about any recent exposure to sick individuals, travel history, and any other symptoms such as fever, cough, or difficulty swallowing. I would also perform a thorough physical examination, paying close attention to the throat, tonsils, and lymph nodes.

Based on the presentation of a sore throat with malaise and fatigue, I would suspect a viral upper respiratory infection (URI) as a possible diagnosis. To confirm this, I would order a rapid antigen detection test (RADT) for Group A Streptococcus (GAS) to rule out streptococcal pharyngitis. If the RADT is negative, I would consider a throat culture to confirm the absence of GAS.

In the meantime, I would provide supportive care, including analgesics for pain management, throat lozenges, and plenty of fluids. I would also educate the patient and his family about the importance of hand hygiene and avoiding close contact with others to prevent the spread of infection. If the patient's symptoms worsen or if the throat culture is positive for GAS, I may consider antibiotic therapy with penicillin or amoxicillin.

Laboratory results:
Na+: 145 mEq/L
K+: 4.0 mEq/L
Cl-: 100 mEq/L
HCO3-: 24 mEq/L
BUN: 12 mg/dL
Ca2+: 10.2 mg/dL
Mg2+: 2.0 mEq/L
Creatinine: 1.0 mg/dL
Glucose: 77 mg/dL
Hemoglobin: 17 g/dL
Hematocrit: 47%
Reticulocyte count: 1.0%
Platelet count: 250,000/mm3
Leukocyte count: 13,000/mm3
Neutrophil: 45%
Lymphocyte: 42%
Monocyte: 12%
Eosinophil: 1%
Basophil: 0%
Physical examination reveals splenomegaly and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy

Based on the laboratory results and physical examination findings, the patient has elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit levels, which may indicate polycythemia vera (PV), a myeloproliferative disorder characterized by an overproduction of red blood cells. The leukocyte count is also elevated, which may be due to the underlying PV or a concurrent infection. The splenomegaly and posterior cervical lymphadenopathy are also consistent with PV.

To confirm the diagnosis of PV, I would order a JAK2 mutation analysis, which is a genetic test that detects the presence of the JAK2 V617F mutation, a common mutation found in patients with PV. I would also consider ordering a complete blood count (CBC) with differential, erythropoietin level, and bone marrow biopsy to further evaluate the patient's hematologic status.

In the meantime, I would provide supportive care, including phlebotomy to reduce the hematocrit level and alleviate symptoms of hyperviscosity. I would also consider low-dose aspirin therapy to reduce the risk of thrombosis. If the JAK2 mutation analysis is positive, I may consider initiating cytoreductive therapy

New patient:

A 20-year-old man, who was previously healthy, is taken to the emergency department due to agitation during the past 24 hours.

To evaluate this patient, I would first take a detailed history, including asking about any recent exposure to illnesses, substance use, and any other symptoms such as fever, headache, or seizures. I would also perform a thorough physical examination, paying close attention to the mental status, vital signs, and neurological examination.

Based on the presentation of agitation and altered mental status, I would suspect a possible central nervous system (CNS) infection, such as meningitis or encephalitis, as a possible diagnosis. To confirm this, I would order a lumbar puncture to obtain cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) for analysis, including cell count, protein, glucose, and culture. I would also consider ordering a CT scan of the head to rule out any structural abnormalities before performing the lumbar puncture.

In the meantime, I would provide supportive care, including intravenous fluids, analgesics for pain management, and antipyretics for fever. If the patient's condition worsens or if the CSF analysis indicates a bacterial infection, I may consider initiating empiric antibiotic therapy with ceftriaxone and vancomycin. If the CSF analysis indicates a viral infection, I may consider antiv

New patient:

A 34-year-old, previously healthy woman is admitted to the hospital with abdominal pain and bloody diarrhea.

To evaluate this patient, I would first take a detailed history, including asking about any recent travel, exposure to sick individuals, and any other symptoms such as fever, vomiting, or weight loss. I would also perform a thorough physical examination, paying close attention to the abdomen, lymph nodes, and skin.

Based on the presentation of abdominal pain and bloody diarrhea, I would suspect a possible infectious gastroenteritis, such as caused by bacteria, viruses, or parasites, as a possible diagnosis. To confirm this, I would order stool cultures, ova and parasite examination, and Clostridium difficile toxin assay. I would also consider ordering blood cultures and a complete blood count (CBC) to evaluate for any systemic infection.

In the meantime, I would provide supportive care, including intravenous fluids for rehydration, analgesics for pain management, and antiemetics for nausea and vomiting. If the stool cultures or ova and parasite examination are positive for a specific pathogen, I may consider initiating targeted antimicrobial therapy. Laboratory results:
Erythrocytes 3 x 106/mm3
Hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL
Hematocrit 0.45 (45%)
Corrected reticulocyte count  5.5%
Platelet count 18,000/mm3
Leukocytes 11,750/mm3
Total bilirubin 2.33 mg/dL (39.8 µmol/L)
Direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL (3.4 µmol/L)
Serum creatinine 4.5 mg/dL (397.8 µmol/L)
Blood urea nitrogen 35.4 mg/dL (12.6 mmol/L)
Vital signs: blood pressure 100/70 mm Hg, respiratory rate 16/min
Direct bilirubin 0
Physical examination shows paleness, face and leg edema, and abdominal tenderness in the lower right quadrant
Her medical history is unremarkable

Based on the laboratory results and physical examination findings, the patient has anemia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated bilirubin levels, which may indicate hemolytic anemia. The presence of abdominal tenderness in the lower right quadrant may suggest an underlying inflammatory or infectious process, such as appendicitis.
